If the file consists of more than one sector, succeeding sectors of the file shall be written to succeeding sectors on the same track.

If the file is larger than one track, succeeding sectors of the file shall be written to succeeding physical tracks on the media.

For example, if a file is 3000 bytes in length and is written using a sector type with a length of 1112 bytes, the data portion of the file will occupy the last 1112 – 36 bytes (the size of the above header) = 1076 bytes of each sector.

This means the file will be written to 3000 / 1076 = 3 sectors (rounding up).

Since the 1112 byte sector type has one sector per track, the file will be written to 3 consecutive physical tracks.

If, for example, the track location for this file in the directory was specified as track 20, then the file will occupy tracks 20, 21, and 22 on the media.

The remaining (3 * 1076) – 3000 = 228 bytes of track 22 shall be written with zeroes and ignored by the application that reads the file.

If there is an error writing one of these logical tracks, the writing application may re-write the data for that track on the next physical track.

title: Partie 5: Format de données pour l’échange d’informations pour les
text:
Because the drive may write a given track and receive an error on the write operation, but later may be able to successfully read the track, the reading application shall be prepared to read and ignore extra copies of the same logical track as it is reading a file.

The last field of the above header contains the byte offset of the first tag within this sector.

This is useful in case this sector contains only part of a TLV stream, the earlier parts of which cannot be read.

In this case, the reader can use the specified byte offset to find the first complete data item in the TLV stream and can then continue parsing the stream without having to have successfully read the earlier sectors.

In cases where the file contains more than one data item, the file data (at offset 36 in each data sector) shall contain the complete TLV stream containing all the data items.

In cases where the file contains a single data item, the file shall contain only the data item itself (the V field) without the T and L fields.

The latter are not required because the tag and length fields are in the directory entry describing the file.

The second field of the header contains a count of the maximum number of tracks that could contain this file.

This is useful in the event of errors reading the file.

It allows the reader to stop looking for tracks if it has already read the last track that could contain part of the file.

title: Partie 5: Format de données pour l’échange d’informations pour les
text: Unique stamp Each normal layout data sector contains a unique stamp. This stamp is different for each file on the card, but the same for all sectors that make up the same file. The reader shall use the unique stamp, along with the other header fields that describe the length of the file and the logical sector number of the sector - to piece together parts of a file that it might read from different locations on a card. For example, if the directory of a card cannot be read, but the reader is able to find all the different logical tracks of the file by scanning all the physical tracks of the card, then the reader will know which piece goes with which file and will be able to paste the files back together. For an example file that was written by the drive with serial number 12345 on March 31, 2002 at 2:59:59 pm and 999 ms, the unique stamp has the following structure and value: Example bytes (hex) 12345 = 3039h 39 30 00 Written with drive with this serial number D2 07 Year 3 = March Month (1 = January) 31 = 31st day 1F Day (from 1 to 31)

title: Partie 5: Format de données pour l’échange d’informations pour les
text: Alternate layout Sometimes it is desired to store a lot of relatively small updates of data that has essentially the same meaning. For example, a passport may contain a ‘stamp’ to record the traveller’s passage into a given country. For such cases, the following alternative layout shall be used: BA EA (hex) Signature for sector containing a single type of TLV entry Tag representing the data to follow Length of data to follow Var “Entered Australia 2001.03.01” Data associated with this tag The above types of TLV entries are expected to occupy succeeding sectors in an area of the media that has its starting track and sector type specified by a single entry in the directory that is associated with the specified tag. Bibliography [1] [2] Framework Price based on 15 pages

title: Partie 5: Format de données pour l’échange d’informations pour les
text: = 2 pm 0E Hour (from 0 = midnight to 23 = 11 pm) 3B Minute (from 0 to 59) 3B Second (from 0 to 59) E7 03 Millisecond (from 0 to 999) Since the time stamp portion of the unique stamp is so precise, the file writer shall use the time just before it started writing the first sector of the file to create the unique stamp which shall be used for all the sectors of the file. This ensures that the unique stamps read from all sectors of the same file are identical.

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text:
CBEFF file biometric data file (a set of bytes) that conforms to ISO/IEC 19785-1 3.3 encoded biometric data biometric data that has been interpreted and encoded 3.4 raw biometric data biometric data obtained directly from a biometric device Interchange of biometric data items directory structure on the card and the method of identifying individual data items that are written to the card.

Using the data format described in ISO/IEC 11694-5, biometric data is accessed in much the same manner as any other type of data on an optical memory card.

This being the case, this part of ISO/IEC 11694 specifies tags and structures which facilitate and which are specific to accessing biometric data items on a card.

Typically, an application that reads a given card looks on that card for tags that correspond to data items that the application knows how to use, based on the published tag document.

ISO/IEC 11694-5 includes a set of defined tag ranges that assist a reading application in accessing useful biometric data on the card in the absence of the application’s knowledge of a particular tag.

The reader requires from the card, images of one or more of the cardholder’s fingerprints.

It queries the card for any data with tags in the range from 7000 through 7999.

Tags in this range correspond to data items containing raw fingerprint images.

If the card contains any such data, the reader can then read the corresponding data item from the card and use it to verify the identity of the cardholder.

The reader can use a process similar to that in the above example to read and use any type of biometric information that is associated with a tag which is not recognized by the reader.

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text:
Biometric tag ranges If a card issuer wishes to write to their cards, biometric data items that do not conform exactly to any of the standards listed in the next section, they shall request from the tag registration authority (as defined in according to the type of biometric item: Tag range Description of biometric items 2000 – 2999 Any encoded (not raw) biometric data that comprises information specifically about the face or head of the subject.

This specifically includes matching of face (visible light or thermal) and ear and excludes matching of eyes.

3000 – 3999 Any encoded (not raw) biometric data that comprises information specifically about the fingers of the subject.

This includes fingerprints and attributes of individual fingers and excludes hand geometry.

4000 – 4999 Any encoded (not raw) biometric data that comprises information specifically about the eyes of the subject.

This specifically includes retina and iris matching.

5000 – 5999 Any encoded (not raw) biometric data that comprises information specifically about the hands or feet of the subject.

This specifically includes hand geometry, palm and foot prints, veins in the hand, and thermal hand image, and excludes fingerprints or individual finger traits, which are in another range.

6000 – 6999 Any raw (not encoded or processed) images of the subject.

This includes the common image of the face (portrait).

7000 – 7999 Any raw (not encoded or processed) images of single digit fingerprints.

8000 – 8999 Any raw images or encoded data that comprises information about the pen-based signature of the subject.

12200 – 12299 Any encoded biometric data that comprises information on a molecular or cellular level.

This specifically includes DNA and body odour matching.

Tag range Description of biometric items 12300 – 12399 Any encoded biometric data that comprises information about a behaviour.

This specifically includes gait, keystroke, lip movement and voice and excludes the pen- based signature, which is in another range.

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text:
12400 – 12799 Any biometric data that does not fit into the above categories and conforms to Framework 12800 – 12899 Any biometric data that does not fit into the above categories and does not conform to Framework A reading application that supports the verification or identification of the cardholder using certain biometric data items can use the above table as a guide, and look for tags in the range that matches its capabilities.

A reading application is in a system that consists of a camera for obtaining the facial image of the cardholder and which contains software that can compare the cardholder’s face against templates assigned to tags 2345, 2346, and certain CBEFF files.

This application can start by querying the card for any data items with tags in the range from 2000 through 2999.

If there are 2345 or 2346 data items, the application can read and use them right away.

If there is an unrecognised data item with tag 2347, the application can look for the CBEFF header for that item and parse that header to see if the item can be used.

Clause 7 describes how to find and read the CBEFF header without having to read the data item itself, if the card issuer supports it.

CBEFF files that meet other standards Although the use of ISO/IEC 19785-1 (CBEFF) is not required by this part of ISO/IEC 11694, the use of existing biometric standards for the storage of biometrics on optical memory cards is strongly recommended.

This part of ISO/IEC 11694 includes specific support for CBEFF files.

The following standards, which all describe CBEFF files, are specific as to which biometric is described, so each has a tag within the tag range corresponding to the type of biometric.

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text:
Exact implementations of these standards shall not require a tag document and shall be assigned the following tags within ISO/IEC 11694-5: Tag Data item meaning minutiae data pattern spectral data image data image data image data Signature/sign time series data pattern skeletal data Finding other relevant CBEFF files The purpose of ISO/IEC 11694-5 is to allow a reader familiar with the standard to read and parse data items corresponding to tags with which the reader is familiar.

The reading application knows how to interpret and parse these items because it was developed using information in the ‘tag document‘ for the tag that is later read from the card.

It is possible that a given tag document may allow for ambiguities that are resolved through the reading of a CBEFF (ISO/IEC 19785-1) header (SBH).

The tag document for a given tag allows for the data item to contain one of two types of biometric data.

The reading system in this example can only make use of one of these types.

The reading application would like to know which of the types is in the data item before it takes the time to read the data item.

It is also possible for an application to search for a useful CBEFF file whose tag was not assigned until after this particular application was deployed to the field.

A reader application has been set up to make use of any CBEFF file that contains the biometric data that it expects, for example Fingerprint Format A.

A card issuer creates a new card that contains a Fingerprint Format A file.

This data item is issued a different tag from the one that our reader application originally used.

In this case, we would like this older reader application to be able to determine that there is a Fingerprint Format A file on the card, and to be able to access that file.

The solution to the cases highlighted in the above two examples is to define a data item that will contain a copy of the SBH for any CBEFF files on the card.

The tag 12900 shall be reserved for such a data item.

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text:
A card issuer can optionally write this data item in order to facilitate the retrieval of a relevant CBEFF file in such cases.

This data item shall consist of one or more header entries.

Each header entry shall consist of a 2-byte tag (as defined in ISO/IEC 11694-5) which identifies the data item containing the CBEFF file, followed by a 2-byte Patron Format Identifier (as defined in ISO/IEC 19785-1) which specifies the format of the SBH, followed by the 2-byte length of the SBH, followed by the SBH itself.

The SBH shall exactly match the Patron file format specified by the Patron Format Identifier in conjunction with the relevant annex of ISO/IEC 19785-1.

Immediately following the SBH in this data item shall be the tag value that represents the beginning of the next header entry (describing another CBEFF file on the card).

This list of header entries shall be terminated by a tag value of zero.

The rest of the terminating header entry need not exist in the data item.

Even in such cases where the SBH portion of a CBEFF file is contained within the CBEFF header item, the corresponding data item (referenced by the tag in the header entry) shall contain the entire CBEFF file, including the SBH portion.

The following table contains an example of a CBEFF header item (tag = 12900) that references two CBEFF files: Offset (bytes) Length (bytes) Example Entry: Field Description 1: T Tag for the data item (e.g.

a face template) 1: P CBEFF Patron Format Identifier (minimum simple byte- oriented patron format in this case) 1: L Length of the header to follow (See -1) 1: H Standard Biometric Header (SBH) for this data item.

If the application finds this CBEFF file useful, the data item itself can be obtained by reading the data item with the tag specified in this entry (in this case 2010).

2: T Tag for the data item (e.g.

an iris template) 2: P CBEFF Patron Format Identifier (bioAPI in this case) 2: L Length of the header to follow (See -1) 2: H Standard Biometric Header (SBH) for this data item.

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text:
If the application finds this CBEFF file useful, the data item itself can be obtained by reading the data item with the tag specified in this entry (in this case 3200).

End NULL Tag marks end of header list In the above cases, the reading application will first look for the tags corresponding to data items it can use based on its being developed with the use of their respective tag documents.

If it does not find such items, but it can make use of other CBEFF files, it will then look for tag 12900.

If that data item is on the card, it will read and parse it, looking for one or more CBEFF headers that correspond to files it can use.

If such a header is found, the application will then read the data item that corresponds to the tag that is found within the entry containing that header, and it then has the relevant CBEFF file.

Bibliography [1] minutiae data [2] pattern spectral data1) [3] image data [4] data [5] data [6] Information technology — Biometric data interchange formats — Part 7: Signature/sign time series data1) [7] pattern skeletal data1) 1) To be published.

Price based on 6 pages

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text: Scope This part of ISO/IEC 11694 describes the use of biometric data on optical memory cards using the logical data structure described in ISO/IEC 11694-5.

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text: Normative references The following documents, in whole or in part, are normatively referenced in this document and are indispensable for its application. For dated references, only the edition cited applies. For undated references, the latest edition of the referenced document (including any amendments) applies. Logical data structures. format for information interchange for applications using ISO/IEC 11694-4 Data element specification data spectral data data data time series data skeletal data

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text: Terms and definitions For the purposes of this document, the terms and definitions given in ISO/IEC 11694-4, ISO/IEC 11694- 5, ISO/IEC 19785-1, and the following, apply. INTERNATIONAL STANDARD  3.1 biometric data set of bytes that describes the physical properties of one or more parts of a living body EXAMPLE The encoded template which mathematically describes a person’s fingerprint. This template can be compared against the fingerprint of the person who is presenting the card.

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text: CBEFF file biometric data file (a set of bytes) that conforms to ISO/IEC 19875‐1 3.3 encoded biometric data biometric data that has been interpreted and encoded 3.4 raw biometric data biometric data obtained directly from a biometric device

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text: Interchange of biometric data items directory structure on the card and the method of identifying individual data items that are written to the card. Using the data format described in ISO/IEC 11694-5, biometric data are accessed in much the same manner as any other type of data on an optical memory card. This being the case, this part of the standard specifies tags and structures which facilitate and which are specific to accessing biometric data items on a card. Typically, an application that reads a given card looks on that card for tags that correspond to data items that the application knows how to use, based on the published tag document. ISO/IEC 11694-5 includes a set of defined tag ranges that assist a reading application in accessing useful biometric data on the card in the absence of the application’s knowledge of a particular tag. EXAMPLE The reader requires from the card, images of one or more of the cardholder’s fingerprints. It queries the card for any data with tags in the range from 7000 through 7999. Tags in this range correspond to data items containing raw fingerprint images. If the card contains any such data, the reader can then read the corresponding data item from the card and use it to verify the identity of the cardholder. The reader can use a process similar to that in the above example to read and use any type of biometric information that is associated with a tag which is not recognized by the reader.

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text:
Biometric Tag Ranges If a card issuer wishes to write to their cards, biometric data items that do not conform exactly to any of the standards listed in the next section, they shall request from the tag registration authority (as defined in Part 5) a new tag.

The tag registration authority shall issue a tag in one of the following ranges, according to the type of biometric item: Tag Range Description of Biometric Items 2000 – 2999 Any encoded (not raw) biometric data that comprises information specifically about the face or head of the subject.

This specifically includes matching of face (visible light or thermal) and ear and excludes matching of eyes.

3000 – 3999 Any encoded (not raw) biometric data that comprises information specifically about the fingers of the subject.

This includes fingerprints and attributes of individual fingers and excludes hand geometry.

4000 – 4999 Any encoded (not raw) biometric data that comprises information specifically about the eyes of the subject.

This specifically includes retina and iris matching.

2  Tag Range Description of Biometric Items 5000 – 5999 Any encoded (not raw) biometric data that comprises information specifically about the hands or feet of the subject.

This specifically includes hand geometry, palm and foot prints, veins in the hand, and thermal hand image, and excludes fingerprints or individual finger traits, which are in another range.

6000 – 6999 Any raw (not encoded or processed) images of the subject.

This includes the common image of the face (portrait).

7000 – 7999 Any raw (not encoded or processed) images of single digit fingerprints.

8000 – 8999 Any raw images or encoded data that comprises information about the pen-based signature of the subject.

12200 – 12299 Any encoded biometric data that comprises information on a molecular or cellular level.

This specifically includes DNA and body odour matching.

12300 – 12399 Any encoded biometric data that comprises information about a behaviour.

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text:
This spe- cifically includes gait, keystroke, lip movement and voice and excludes the pen-based signature, which is in another range.

12400 – 12799 Any biometric data that does not fit into the above categories and conforms to ISO/ IEC 19785-1, Information Technology – Common Biometric Exchange Formats Frame‐ work 12800 – 12899 Any biometric data that does not fit into the above categories and does not conform to ISO/IEC 19785-1, Information Technology – Common Biometric Exchange Formats Framework A reading application that supports the verification or identification of the cardholder using certain biometric data items can use the above table as a guide, and look for tags in the range that matches its capabilities.

EXAMPLE A reading application is in a system that consists of a camera for obtaining the facial image of the cardholder and which contains software that can compare the cardholder’s face against templates assigned to tags 2345, 2346, and certain CBEFF files.

This application can start by querying the card for any data items with tags in the range from 2000 through 2999.

If there are 2345 or 2346 data items, the application can read and use them right away.

If there is an unrecognized data item with tag 2347, the application can look for the CBEFF header for that item and parse that header to see if the item can be used.

Section 7 describes how to find and read the CBEFF header without having to read the data item itself, if the card issuer supports it.

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
chapter_title: CBEFF files that meet other standards
text: CBEFF files that meet other standards Although the use of ISO/IEC  19875-1 (CBEFF) is not required by this standard, the use of existing biometric standards for the storage of biometrics on optical memory cards is strongly recommended. This part of ISO/IEC 11694 includes specific support for CBEFF files. The following standards, which all describe CBEFF files, are specific as to which biometric is described, so each has a tag within the tag range corresponding to the type of biometric. Exact implementations of these standards shall not require a tag document and shall be assigned the following tags within Tag Data Item Meaning minutiae data pattern spectral data image data image data Tag Data Item Meaning image data ture/Sign Behavioural Data Pattern Skeletal Data

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text:
Finding other relevant CBEFF files The purpose of ISO/IEC 11694-5 is to allow a reader familiar with the standard to read and parse data items corresponding to tags with which the reader is familiar.

The reading application knows how to interpret and parse these items because it was developed using information in the ‘tag document‘ for the tag that is later read from the card.

It is possible that a given tag document may allow for ambiguities that are resolved through the reading of a CBEFF (ISO/IEC 19785-1) header (SBH).

EXAMPLE The tag document for a given tag allows for the data item to contain one of two types of biometric data.

The reading system in this example can only make use of one of these types.

The reading application would like to know which of the types is in the data item before it takes the time to read the data item.

It is also possible for an application to search for a useful CBEFF file whose tag was not assigned until after this particular application was deployed to the field.

EXAMPLE A reader application has been set up to make use of any CBEFF file that contains the biometric data that it expects, for example Fingerprint Format A.

A card issuer creates a new card that contains a Fingerprint Format A file.

This data item is issued a different tag from the one that our reader application originally used.

In this case, we would like this older reader application to be able to determine that there is a Fingerprint Format A file on the card, and to be able to access that file.

The solution to the cases highlighted in the above two examples is to define a data item that will contain a copy of the SBH for any CBEFF files on the card.

The tag 12900 shall be reserved for such a data item.

A card issuer can optionally write this data item in order to facilitate the retrieval of a relevant CBEFF file in such cases.

This data item shall consist of one or more header entries.

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text:
Each header entry shall consist of a 2-byte tag (as defined in ISO/IEC 11694-5) which identifies the data item containing the CBEFF file, followed by a 2-byte Patron Format Identifier (as defined in ISO/IEC 19785-1) which specifies the format of the SBH, followed by the 2-byte length of the SBH, followed by the SBH itself.

The SBH shall exactly match the Patron file format specified by the Patron Format Identifier in conjunction with the relevant annex of the beginning of the next header entry (describing another CBEFF file on the card).

This list of header entries shall be terminated by a tag value of zero.

The rest of the terminating header entry need not exist in the data item.

Even in such cases where the SBH portion of a CBEFF file is contained within the CBEFF header item, the corresponding data item (referenced by the tag in the header entry) shall contain the entire CBEFF file, including the SBH portion.

EXAMPLE The following table contains an example of a CBEFF header item (tag = 12900) that references two CBEFF files: Offset (Bytes) Length (Bytes) Example Entry: Field Description 1: T Tag for the data item (e.g.

a face template) 4  Offset (Bytes) Length (Bytes) Example Entry: Field Description 1: P CBEFF Patron Format Identi- fier (minimum simple byte- oriented patron format in this case) 1: L Length of the header to follow (See ISO/IEC 19785-1) 1: H Standard Biometric Header (SBH) for this data item.

If the application finds this CBEFF file useful, the data item itself can be obtained by reading the data item with the tag specified in this entry (in this case 2010).

2: T Tag for the data item (e.g.

an iris template) 2: P CBEFF Patron Format Identi- fier (bioAPI in this case) 2: L Length of the header to follow (See ISO/IEC 19785-1) 2: H Standard Biometric Header (SBH) for this data item.

If the application finds this CBEFF file useful, the data item itself can be obtained by reading the data item with the tag specified in this entry (in this case 3200).

title: Partie 6: Emploi de la biométrie sur une carte à mémoire optique
text:
End NULL Tag marks end of header list In the above cases, the reading application will first look for the tags corresponding to data items it can use based on its being developed with the use of their respective tag documents.

If it does not find such items, but it can make use of other CBEFF files, it will then look for tag 12900.

If that data item is on the card, it will read and parse it, looking for one or more CBEFF headers that correspond to files it can use.

If such a header is found, the application will then read the data item that corresponds to the tag that is found within the entry containing that header, and it then has the relevant CBEFF file.
